- optimizer: Read if asked to read the optimizer skill
- shepherd: How to organize a population of search agents
- explorer: Read if asked to read the explorer skill
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Pause and reflect on existing findings, if any. Come up with a fundamentally new idea for an improved solution. Do NOT make small, incremental tweaks. 

**STEP 3: Implement and evaluate one idea**

- Use the standard evaluator for evaluating your solution
- After *every* evaluation, EXPLICITLY DECIDE whether to continue iterating or finish up. We have limited budget so be conservative with your iterations. If you have fully implemented a single idea, then finish up. Don't branch out into other ideas that majorly deviate from the initial idea. If the result suggests a targeted fix or improvement, you may continue editing initial_program.py and run ./task-eval again. If the idea doesn't work after a few attempts, give up immediately. Finish up by creating/editing findings.md as needed and committing your changes."

**Step 4: Create/edit findings.md as needed**
- `findings.md` concisely records failures and successes. It is strictly factual and neutral. 
- Only make edits if you learned new facts. Otherwise, keep as is.

IMPORTANT GUARDRAIL: Do NOT discuss next steps or suggestions on what is necessary to improve the score. Simply record the basic facts of your solution and its evaluation. 

**Step 5: Commit all your changes**
- Commit message should follow this format: `{concise experiment description} | score = {score from standard evaluator or concise failure reason}`. 
  - Do not include a score on failure. Only include a description of the failure reason.

When returning a summary back to the orchestrator, include your commit emssage and the commit hash.

content.content: 1 You are a world-class algorithm engineer, and you are very good at programming. Now, you are participating in a programming contest. You are asked to solve a heuristic problem, known as an NP-hard problem. 2 3 Story 4 -------- 5 AtCoder's CEO, Takahashi, loves animals and has a number of pets running free in the AtCoder office. 6 AtCoder's employees have trouble with the pets interrupting their work, so they have decided to place partitions in the office to create a space where pets cannot come in. 7 Please create as large a space as possible. 8 9 Problem Statement 10 -------- 11 There are $N$ pets and $M$ people in a room with a floor of $30 \times 30$ squares. 12 All squares are initially passable, and outside of the $30 \times 30$ squares are impassable. 13 Let $(x, y)$ be the coordinates of the square in row $x$ from the top ($1\leq x\leq 30$) and column $y$ from the left ($1\leq y\leq 30$). 14 Repeat the following process for $300$ turns. 15 16 First, you choose each person's action from the following three types, and perform each action simultaneously. 17 18 - Do nothing and stay in the current position. 19 - Choose a square adjacent to the current position and make it impassable. You cannot choose a square that contains pets or humans at the start of this turn. You cannot choose a square whose adjacent square contains a pet, either. If you choose a square that is already impassable, nothing happens. 20 - Move to an adjacent passable square. It is not possible to move to a square that becomes impassable by another person's action in this turn. 21 22 After all the people have completed their actions for that turn, each pet moves independently. 23 Rules for pet movement depend on the type of pet, and some pets may move multiple squares in a single turn. 24 Details are described later. 25 26 Squares containing humans or pets are also passable, and each square can contain any number of humans and pets. 27 28 29 Scoring 30 -------- 31 At the end of $300$ turn, for each $i=1,\cdots,M$, let $R_i$ be the set of squares reachable from the final position of person $i$ through only passable squares, and $n_i$ be the number of pets whose final position is in $R_i$. 32 Then, person $i$ obtains satisfaction of $s_i=\frac{|R_i|}{900}\times 2^{-n_i}$. 33 The score for the test case is $\mathrm{round}\left(10^8\times\frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^M s_i\right)$. 34 35 #### Number of test cases 36 - Provisional test: 100 37 - System test: 2000. We will publish seeds.txt (md5=27bf0702bbe0265900374c3b6b9846b4, sha256=33973e4ded08e3a607fc2e841e14751ff110ae10154b286e7fd5f766ff86d706) after the contest is over. 38 39 The score of a submission is the total scores for each test case. 40 In the provisional test, if your submission produces illegal output or exceeds the time limit for some test cases, the submission itself will be judged as WA or TLE , and the score of the submission will be zero. 41 In the system test, if your submission produces illegal output or exceeds the time limit for some test cases, only the score for those test cases will be zero. 42 Note that if your program terminates abnormally, it may be judged as WA instead of RE. 43 44 #### About execution time 45 Execution time may vary slightly from run to run. 46 In addition, since system tests simultaneously perform a large number of executions, it has been observed that execution time increases by several percent compared to provisional tests. 47 For these reasons, submissions that are very close to the time limit may result in TLE in the system test. 48 Please measure the execution time in your program to terminate the process, or have enough margin in the execution time. 49 50 51 Input and Output 52 -------- 53 First, the initial position and type of each pet, and the initial position of each person are given from Standard Input in the following format 54 ~~~ 55 $N$ 56 $px_1$ $py_1$ $pt_1$ 57 $\vdots$ 58 $px_N$ $py_N$ $pt_N$ 59 $M$ 60 $hx_1$ $hy_1$ 61 $\vdots$ 62 $hx_M$ $hy_M$ 63 ~~~ 64 $N$ is an integer between $10$ and $20$ representing the number of pets. 65 $(px_i,py_i)$ represents the coordinates of the initial position of the $i$-th pet, and $pt_i$ is an integer between $1$ and $5$ representing the type of the $i$-th pet. 66 $M$ is an integer between $5$ and $10$ representing the number of humans. 67 $(hx_i,hy_i)$ represents the coordinates of the initial position of the $i$-th human. 68 The initial positions of all pets and humans are guaranteed to be distinct. 69 70 After reading the above information, repeat the following process $300$ turns. 71 72 First, output a string of length $M$ where the $i$-th character represents the action of the $i$th person as follows on a single line to Standard Output. 73 **After the output, you have to flush Standard Output.** Otherwise, the submission might be judged as TLE . 74 75 - `.`: Do nothing and stay in the current position. 76 - `u`, `d`, `l`, `r`: Let $(x,y)$ be the current position. Make the square $(x-1,y)$, $(x+1,y)$, $(x,y-1)$, or $(x,y+1)$ impassable, respectively. 77 - `U`, `D`, `L`, `R`: Let $(x,y)$ be the current position. Move to the the square $(x-1,y)$, $(x+1,y)$, $(x,y-1)$, or $(x,y+1)$, respectively. 78 79 After the output, $N$ strings are given to Standard Input in a single line, separated by spaces. 80 The $i$-th string represents movement of the $i$-th pet in that turn. 81 If the pet does not move, the string is `.`. 82 If it does move, the string is a sequence of characters `U`, `D`, `L`, and `R` representing the movement of one square up, down, left, and right, respectively. 83 84 Show example 85 86 87 Pets Movement Rules 88 -------- 89 We define a basic move as follows: move to a square chosen at random among the adjacent passable squares. From the condition of the squares that can be made impassable, such squares always exist. 90 91 Each pet $i$ performs the following moves depending on $pt_i$, an integer value between $1$ and $5$ representing its type. 92 93 1. Cow: Perform one basic move. 94 2. Pig: Perform two basic moves. 95 3. Rabbit: Perform three basic moves. 96 4. Dog: Move toward a target person as follows. The first turn starts with no target. If it has no target, the target person is in the current position, or there exists no path to the target person, then it selects one person uniformly at random among those reachable from the current position, excluding those in the current position. If there is no such person, reset to no target and perform one basic move. Otherwise, move to an adjacent passable square that shortens the shortest distance to the target person (if there are multiple such squares, choose one of them uniformly at random), and then perform one basic move. If it reaches the destination after the first or the second move, reset to no target. 97 5. Cat: Move toward a target square as follows. The first turn starts with no target. If it has no target or there exists no path to the target square, then it selects one square uniformly at random among those reachable from the current position, excluding the current position. If there exists no such square, do nothing. Otherwise, move to an adjacent passable square that shortens the shortest distance to the target square (if there are multiple such squares, choose one of them uniformly at random), and then perform one basic move. If it reaches the destination after the first or the second move, reset to no target. 98 99 100 Input Generation 101 -------- 102 Let $\mathrm{rand}(L,U)$ be a function that generates a uniform random integer between $L$ and $U$, inclusive. 103 104 We generate the number of pets by $N=\mathrm{rand}(10, 20)$. 105 The initial position of each pet is chosen uniformly at random from the coordinates that have not been chosen yet. 106 We generate the type of each pet by $pt_i=\mathrm{rand}(1, 5)$. 107 108 We generate the number of humans by $M=\mathrm{rand}(5, 10)$. 109 The initial position of each human is chosen uniformly at random from the coordinates that have not been chosen yet. 110 111 112 Tools 113 -------- 114 - Local tester: You need a compilation environment of Rust language. 115 - For those who are not familiar with the Rust language environment, we have prepared a pre-compiled binary for Windows. tools_x86_64-pc-windows-gnu.zip 116 - The first version contained a bug in the cat's movement, which has been fixed at 130 minutes after the contest started. Please re-download it. 117 - We have added more examples in README. If you don't know how to use the tools, please refer to README. Also, as stated in the rules, you are free to share information on how to run the provided tools. 118 - Web visualizer: By pasting the output generated by the local tester into the Output field, you can display the animation of the execution result. 119 120 You are allowed to share output images (png or gif) of the provided visualizer for seed=0 on twitter during the contest. You have to use the specified hashtag and public account. You can only share visualization results and scores for seed=0. Do not share scores for other seeds or mention solutions or discussions. List of shared images. 121 122 #### Specification of input/output files used by the tools 123 Input files for the local tester consist of the prior information (the initial position and type of each pet, and the initial position of each person) followed by a random seed value to generate pet movements. 124 Since the pet's movement depends on human actions, the input file contains only the random seed value and not specific movements. 125 The local tester writes outputs from your program directly to the output file. 126 Your program may output comment lines starting with `#`. 127 The web version of the visualizer displays the comment lines at the time they are output, which may be useful for debugging and analysis. 128 Since the judge program ignores all comment lines, you can submit a program that outputs comment lines as is. 129 130 Problem constraints: 131 time_limit=3.0 memory_limit=1073741824 132 133 Evaluation: 134 - Run `./task-eval` 135 - The evaluation timeout is 10000 seconds

content.thinking:
Let me understand the current situation:

1. The problem is an AHC (AtCoder Heuristic Contest) problem where we need to place partitions in a 30x30 grid to create safe spaces for humans away from pets.
2. The current solution divides the grid into horizontal strips and assigns each human to build walls around their strip.
3. The scoring rewards large reachable areas for humans with few or no pets in those areas.

The current approach has several issues:
- It divides the grid into horizontal strips, which means each human has to build a LOT of walls (the perimeter of their strip)
- With 300 turns and 5-10 humans, each human can only build ~300 walls at most (if they never need to move)


- The strips span the full width, creating massive perimeters, and there's no coordination to actually trap the pets—each human just tries to wall off their own area independently.

I'm realizing the scoring formula heavily penalizes having even a single pet in your area, so the real win is getting a large pet-free zone. Instead of each human building their own enclosure, what if all humans collaborated to construct one large shared enclosure that excludes all pets? That would be far more efficient with the limited wall-building budget.

The key constraint is that you can't build walls adjacent to pets, which makes it hard to corral them directly. So the better strategy is to identify a region of the grid with few or no pets currently, then have everyone work together to build a perimeter around it—using the grid edges where possible to minimize the walls needed.

With a corner-based approach, you'd only need to build two walls. For instance, a 30×30 safe zone in a corner requires just 60 walls total, and with five humans over 300 turns, you could build far more than that, leaving plenty of capacity for a much larger protected area.

The real challenge is timing the wall placement around moving pets—you can't build adjacent to them, so you'd need to coordinate when the area is clear. I'm thinking the most practical approach is a simple collaborative strategy where all humans work together to build a single dividing line across the grid, then everyone moves to whichever side has fewer pets.

Let me understand the current solution and think about a fundamentally new approach.

The current solution:
1. Divides the 30x30 grid into horizontal strips (one per human)
2. Each human builds walls around their strip's borders
3. After walls are built, humans move to a safe spot in the center of their strip
4. The scoring is based on reachable area from each person's final position, penalized by pets in that area

Problems with the current approach:
1. It divides the grid into strips spanning the full width (30), which creates very large areas that are hard to fully wall off in 300 turns


2. Wall construction is inefficient—each human builds their strip's perimeter without coordinating to actually contain pets
3. The wall assignment logic overlaps awkwardly, with the first human handling the top, the last handling the bottom, and intermediate humans splitting their sections
4. With 5-10 humans each building 30-wide strips, the perimeter becomes massive relative to the 300 turns available

A better strategy would be to build small, efficient safe rooms instead of sprawling enclosures. A compact room like a 3x3 interior surrounded by walls would require far fewer turns to complete and would be much easier to defend.

Looking at the scoring formula |R_i|/900 * 2^(-n_i), I realize that even a small enclosed area with zero pets guarantees positive points. A 3x3 room yields about 0.01 per person, while a 5x5 room gives roughly 0.028. The real optimization is positioning these rooms near corners or edges to maximize enclosed area while minimizing the walls needed to build them.

With 300 turns and 5-10 humans available, each person can construct roughly 150 walls if they alternate between moving and building, though the actual number is lower due to positioning constraints. The strategic question becomes whether to pursue larger, harder-to-build areas or smaller, guaranteed-safe rooms that are quicker to construct.

I'm considering a collaborative approach instead: what if all the humans work together to build a single dividing wall across the grid, splitting it into two halves and trying to keep one half completely pet-free? This could be more efficient than individual enclosed areas.

But I'm realizing the fundamental issues with this strategy—pets might already be scattered across both sides, and the constraint about not building adjacent to pets makes it hard to execute. Plus, the current horizontal strip approach has its own problems: the strips share borders which complicates wall assignments, and there's significant travel time overhead just to build the walls in the first place.

A better approach might be to have each person build their own small sealed room positioned strategically to avoid pets. Since there are 300 turns available, one person can construct a complete enclosure and get inside it. The key advantage is that the grid boundary is already impassable, so a room in a corner only needs one or two walls to seal off a small area—much more efficient than trying to divide the entire grid. right wall, plus a corner wall at (k+1, k+1) to fully seal it off. That gives me 2k+1 walls total for a k×k area. Looking at the math: a 5×5 area needs 11 walls for 25 passable squares, a 10×10 needs 21 walls for 100 squares, and a 14×14 needs 29 walls for 196 squares. # Findings

## Approach: Cooperative horizontal wall-line strategy (single and dual wall)

### Strategy
Instead of each human independently building their own rectangular enclosure (original approach), all humans cooperate to build one or two complete horizontal wall lines across the grid. The 30x30 grid boundaries serve as free walls on three sides, so a single horizontal line creates a sealed zone.

The dual-wall variant builds two horizontal lines, creating a "pet cage" in the middle. Humans above the top wall and below the bottom wall each have large pet-free zones.

### Key decisions
- **Wall row selection**: Evaluated all pairs of wall rows by computing expected score (area/900 * 2^(-pets)) for both safe zones, penalized by average human distance to wall.
- **Single vs dual wall**: Compared single-wall score vs dual-wall score. Only chose single wall if it's 10%+ better.
- **Human assignment**: For dual wall, humans are greedily assigned to top/bot wall based on proximity preference.
- **Building**: Humans stand on the safe side of their assigned wall and build toward the wall. Greedy column selection picks nearest unbuilt column.
- **After building**: Humans move to center of their safe zone.

### Results

| Version | Overall Score | Per-case Avg |
|---------|-------------|--------------|
| Original (independent strips) | ~132.7M | ~2.65M |
| Single wall cooperative | ~144.3M | ~2.89M |
| Dual wall cooperative | **185.2M** | **~3.70M** |

### Observations
- Cooperative wall building is far more efficient than independent enclosures since 30 cells of wall creates 30*N cells of area using boundary walls.
- Dual walls work better because they trap pets in the middle, giving both top and bottom groups nearly pet-free zones.
- The scoring formula heavily penalizes pets in the zone (factor of 2^(-n)), so isolating all pets between two walls is very effective.

# Findings

## Approach: Cooperative horizontal wall-line strategy (single and dual wall)

### Strategy
Instead of each human independently building their own rectangular enclosure (original approach), all humans cooperate to build one or two complete horizontal wall lines across the grid. The 30x30 grid boundaries serve as free walls on three sides, so a single horizontal line creates a sealed zone.

The dual-wall variant builds two horizontal lines, creating a "pet cage" in the middle. Humans above the top wall and below the bottom wall each have large pet-free zones.

### Key decisions
- **Wall row selection**: Evaluated all pairs of wall rows by computing expected score (area/900 * 2^(-pets)) for both safe zones, penalized by average human distance to wall.
- **Single vs dual wall**: Compared single-wall score vs dual-wall score. Only chose single wall if it's 10%+ better.
- **Human assignment**: For dual wall, humans are greedily assigned to top/bot wall based on proximity preference.
- **Building**: Humans stand on the safe side of their assigned wall and build toward the wall. Greedy column selection picks nearest unbuilt column.
- **After building**: Humans move to center of their safe zone.

### Results

| Version | Overall Score | Per-case Avg |
|---------|-------------|--------------|
| Original (independent strips) | ~132.7M | ~2.65M |
| Single wall cooperative | ~144.3M | ~2.89M |
| Dual wall cooperative | **185.2M** | **~3.70M** |

### Observations
- Cooperative wall building is far more efficient than independent enclosures since 30 cells of wall creates 30*N cells of area using boundary walls.
- Dual walls work better because they trap pets in the middle, giving both top and bottom groups nearly pet-free zones.
- The scoring formula heavily penalizes pets in the zone (factor of 2^(-n)), so isolating all pets between two walls is very effective.

input.content:
# Findings

## Approach: Cooperative horizontal wall-line strategy (single and dual wall)

### Strategy
Instead of each human independently building their own rectangular enclosure (original approach), all humans cooperate to build one or two complete horizontal wall lines across the grid. The 30x30 grid boundaries serve as free walls on three sides, so a single horizontal line creates a sealed zone.

The dual-wall variant builds two horizontal lines, creating a "pet cage" in the middle. Humans above the top wall and below the bottom wall each have large pet-free zones.

### Key decisions
- **Wall row selection**: Evaluated all pairs of wall rows by computing expected score (area/900 * 2^(-pets)) for both safe zones, penalized by estimated build time.
- **Single vs dual wall**: Compared single-wall score vs dual-wall score. Only chose single wall if it's 10%+ better.
- **Human assignment**: For dual wall, humans are greedily assigned to top/bot wall based on proximity preference. The split ratio is optimized across all possible splits (not just M/2).
- **Building**: Humans stand on the safe side of their assigned wall and build toward the wall. Greedy column selection picks nearest unbuilt column.
- **After building**: Humans move to center of their safe zone.

### Results

| Version | Overall Score | Per-case Avg |
|---------|-------------|--------------|
| Original (independent strips) | ~132.7M | ~2.65M |
| Single wall cooperative | ~144.3M | ~2.89M |
| Dual wall cooperative | ~185.2M | ~3.70M |
| Dual wall + optimized split ratio | **211.5M** | **~4.23M** |

### Failed experiments
- **Walk-and-build with contiguous ranges**: Assigning contiguous column ranges and building sequentially performed worse (125.6M). Caused coordination issues.
- **Opportunistic building**: Building any adjacent unbuilt wall cell before moving to target (203.6M). Hurt because humans sometimes build from wrong side or interfere with other humans' targets.
- **Help-other-wall after own wall done**: Humans crossing to help build the other wall (141.1M). Very harmful because humans traverse the cage and get trapped with pets.
- **Widened wall row search (2-29, min sep 3)**: Expanding search range slightly reduced score (205.7M). Extreme wall positions give too little safe area to be worth it.

### Observations
- Cooperative wall building is far more efficient than independent enclosures since 30 cells of wall creates 30*N cells of area using boundary walls.
- Dual walls work better because they trap pets in the middle, giving both top and bottom groups nearly pet-free zones.
- The scoring formula heavily penalizes pets in the zone (factor of 2^(-n)), so isolating all pets between two walls is very effective.
- Optimizing the human split ratio (not just M/2 vs M/2) across all pairs is very impactful (+14% score).
- Humans must stay on their safe side at all times. Any scheme that sends humans through the cage area is harmful.

input.content:
# Findings

## Approach: Cooperative horizontal wall-line strategy (single and dual wall)

### Strategy
Instead of each human independently building their own rectangular enclosure (original approach), all humans cooperate to build one or two complete horizontal wall lines across the grid. The 30x30 grid boundaries serve as free walls on three sides, so a single horizontal line creates a sealed zone.

The dual-wall variant builds two horizontal lines, creating a "pet cage" in the middle. Humans above the top wall and below the bottom wall each have large pet-free zones.

### Key decisions
- **Wall row selection**: Evaluated all pairs of wall rows (tr=3..20, br=tr+4..28) by computing expected score (area/900 * 2^(-pets)) for both safe zones, penalized by estimated build time with coefficient 0.025.
- **Single vs dual wall**: Compared single-wall score vs dual-wall score using unified time penalty formula. Only chose single wall if it's 10%+ better.
- **Human assignment**: For dual wall, humans are greedily assigned to top/bot wall based on proximity preference. The split ratio is optimized across all possible splits (not just M/2).
- **Building**: Humans stand on the safe side of their assigned wall and build toward the wall. Greedy column selection picks nearest unbuilt column.
- **After building**: Humans move to center of their safe zone.

### Results

| Version | Overall Score | Per-case Avg |
|---------|-------------|--------------|
| Original (independent strips) | ~132.7M | ~2.65M |
| Single wall cooperative | ~144.3M | ~2.89M |
| Dual wall cooperative | ~185.2M | ~3.70M |
| Dual wall + optimized split ratio | ~211.5M | ~4.23M |
| + time penalty coeff 0.025 | ~214.5M | ~4.29M |
| + unified single-wall penalty | **219.1M** | **~4.38M** |

### Failed experiments
- **Walk-and-build with contiguous ranges**: Assigning contiguous column ranges and building sequentially performed worse (125.6M). Caused coordination issues.
- **Opportunistic building**: Building any adjacent unbuilt wall cell before moving to target (203.6M). Hurt because humans sometimes build from wrong side or interfere with other humans' targets.
- **Help-other-wall after own wall done**: Humans crossing to help build the other wall (141.1M). Very harmful because humans traverse the cage and get trapped with pets.
- **Widened wall row search (2-29, min sep 3)**: Expanding search range slightly reduced score (205.7M). Extreme wall positions give too little safe area.
- **Move sideways when pet-blocked**: Instead of waiting, moving to next unbuilt column (200.7M). Wasted turns traveling; pets typically move away quickly.
- **Time penalty 0.035**: Overshot optimal (213.5M vs 214.5M at 0.025).

### Observations
- Cooperative wall building is far more efficient than independent enclosures since 30 cells of wall creates 30*N cells of area using boundary walls.
- Dual walls work better because they trap pets in the middle, giving both top and bottom groups nearly pet-free zones.
- The scoring formula heavily penalizes pets in the zone (factor of 2^(-n)), so isolating all pets between two walls is very effective.
- Optimizing the human split ratio (not just M/2 vs M/2) across all pairs is very impactful (+14% score).
- Humans must stay on their safe side at all times. Any scheme that sends humans through the cage area is harmful.
- Time penalty coefficient of 0.025 is near-optimal for the evaluation formula. It correctly weights speed of wall completion.
- When blocked by a pet, waiting (doing nothing) is better than moving to another column - the pet will likely move away next turn.
- Using a consistent time penalty formula for both single-wall and dual-wall decisions improves the selection quality.
